preFix.title: Dyeing with acorns and oak bark
preFix.subtitle: Alum-mordanted wool dyed with acorn caps and oak bark to produce warm tan to soft khaki shades
preFix.excerpt: Acorn caps and oak bark are both high in tannin, making them reliable natural dye materials for warm tan to soft khaki shades on alum-mordanted wool. The two plant parts give slightly different results: acorn caps produce a warmer, redder tan, while oak bark alone shifts toward a greyer khaki. An iron after-bath pushes the colour further into a muted olive-green. This tutorial follows traditional European tannin-dyeing practice.
preFix.sourceNotes: Traditional European tannin-dyeing practice. Pellew, C.O., 'Dyes and Dyeing' (1913), references oak-gall and acorn-cup mordanting. Alum percentages and temperature ranges consistent with UK natural dye guild instruction.

content.text: Acorn caps and oak bark are among the most historically documented natural dye materials in Europe. Both parts of the oak are rich in
tannin
, which bonds readily to wool protein and produces warm tan to soft khaki shades when combined with an alum
mordant
. Mordant: alum potash at 15%
weight of fibre
(WOF). Acorn caps give a warmer, slightly reddish tan; oak bark alone produces a cooler, greyer khaki. An
iron modifier
after-bath shifts the result to a muted olive-green. Both materials are gathered for free in autumn from British woodlands and hedgerows.

attrs.body: Work outdoors or with an extractor fan. Dedicated pots only. Gloves, apron, closed-toe shoes. Keep children and pets out of the dye area.
attrs.title: Mordant safety

items.name: Dried acorn caps or small oak bark pieces (or a mix of both)

content.text: Method
Step 1: Pre-wet the fibre

Soak the dry wool in warm water for at least 30 minutes. The fibre must be fully saturated before entering the mordant or dye bath. Dry spots will resist colour and produce uneven results. Set the pre-wetted fibre aside in a bowl of warm water while you prepare the mordant bath.
Step 2: Mordant bath

Dissolve 15 g of alum potash in a cup of hot water, then add to the dye pot with 4 litres of water. Stir well. Lower the pre-wetted wool into the pot and heat slowly to 80°C over 30 minutes, stirring gently every 10 minutes. Hold at 80°C for 45 minutes. Remove the wool and allow it to cool in the mordant liquor. Do not wring or shock with cold water. Store the mordant liquor: it can be used again for another batch at a reduced dose. Pour spent alum mordant bath down a foul drain. Alum at home-dyeing quantities is low hazard, but it should not enter storm drains or watercourses.
Step 3: Dye-plant preparation

Place the dried acorn caps or oak bark pieces in a separate pot with 4 litres of cold water. Bring slowly to a simmer and hold at 85 to 90°C for 1 hour. The water will turn a deep amber-brown. Strain the plant material out through a cloth or old pillowcase and discard the solids. Return the strained dye liquor to the dye pot.
Step 4: Dye bath

Lower the mordanted wool into the strained dye liquor. Heat to 80°C over 20 minutes and hold at 80°C for 45 minutes, stirring gently every 10 minutes. The wool will absorb colour steadily throughout the hold time. Remove the pot from the heat and allow the wool to cool in the dye bath for at least 30 minutes before lifting. Cooling in the bath deepens the colour slightly.
Step 5: Rinse and dry

Lift the wool from the dye bath and rinse in water that is close to the temperature of the bath, then rinse again in progressively cooler water until the water runs clear. Squeeze gently and hang in a shaded spot to dry. Avoid direct sunlight while drying. The warm tan colour deepens as the fibre dries.
Colour variations

Alum mordant with acorn caps produces a warm reddish tan. Alum mordant with oak bark alone gives a cooler soft khaki with a slight grey cast. A mix of acorn caps and oak bark produces a balanced mid-tan.

Iron after-bath: after the main dye bath, dissolve 2 g of iron sulphate in a cup of hot water and add to a small pot of warm water. Transfer the freshly dyed, still-warm wool into the iron bath and hold at 60 to 70°C for 15 minutes. The colour shifts from warm tan to a muted olive-green. Rinse thoroughly after the iron bath.

Pour spent iron mordant bath down a foul drain only. Never pour into a storm drain, a stream, a garden soak-away, or any watercourse. Iron sulphate in accumulated quantities is harmful to aquatic life.

No mordant (tannin only): because acorn caps and oak bark are high in tannin, they produce a pale straw-tan on un-mordanted wool. The tannin itself has some affinity for protein fibre. The colour is noticeably lighter than the alum-mordanted result and has lower washfastness.
Troubleshooting

items.fix: Use 150 g of plant material per 100 g of fibre for a richer result. Confirm the alum dose was 15% WOF (15 g per 100 g of dry fibre). A second dip in the dye bath after the first rinse will deepen the colour.
items.cause: The mordant percentage was too low, or the dye bath concentration was too weak
items.problem: The colour is very pale after drying despite a deep tan in the bath

items.fix: Ensure no dry spots remain before entering any bath. Stir every 10 minutes during both mordanting and dyeing, lifting the fibre gently to allow the liquid to circulate.
items.cause: The fibre was not fully pre-wetted, or was not stirred regularly during the mordant and dye baths
items.problem: The colour is uneven with pale patches

items.fix: Rinse thoroughly after dyeing until the water runs clear. For the first wash after the piece is finished, use cool water only and a small amount of pH-neutral wool wash. Some colour release in the first wash is normal with tannin-rich plants.
items.cause: Excess tannin on the surface of the fibre that did not bond
items.problem: The colour bleeds heavily in the first wash
